coredata.dc:title: Design and preparation of SnO2/SnS2/conjugated polyvinyl chloride derivative ternary composite with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Yan
Wang, Caixia
Zhang, Yongcai
Yang, Zhanjun
Dionysiou, Dionysios D.
coredata.dc:description: A new high-efficiency ternary composite photocatalyst (SnO2/SnS2/conjugated polyvinyl chloride derivative (CPVC)) was designed based on the hypothesis that any two of its components can constitute type-II heterojunction, and its three components should have synergistic effect on the enhancement of photoinduced charge separation. SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite was prepared adopting an easy-to-implement four-step synthesis method. Photoluminescence and electrochemical impedance spectra showed that SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is more efficient than SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC and SnS2 in the separation and transfer of photoinduced charges. Results from their photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under visible-light irradiation indicated that SnO2/SnS2/CPVC has significantly higher visible-light photocatalytic activity than SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, SnS2, CPVC and SnO2. Moreover, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC also demonstrated good photocatalytic reusability and stability. This study not only has developed a new high-activity visible-light photocatalyst (SnO2/SnS2/CPVC), but is also useful for the design of other new ternary composite photocatalysts with synergistically enhanced visible-light photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Composites
A. Nanostructures
B. Chemical synthesis
C. Raman spectroscopy
D. Catalytic properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic enhancement mechanisms for (a) SnO2/SnS2 [20], (b) SnS2/CPVC [21], (c) SnO2/CPVC [22], and (d) SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.
The determined band structures of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC.

(a) XRD spectra of the as-prepared CPVC, SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2 and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (b) FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC and CPVC; (c) Raman spectra of the as-prepared SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2 and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.
(a) TEM image of the as-prepared SnO2; (b) TEM image of the as-prepared SnS2; (c) TEM image of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (d) HRTEM image of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.
(a) UV-vis absorbance spectra of SnO2, SnS2, CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (b) Tauc plots of SnO2, SnS2 and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (c) Tauc plot of CPVC.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.

(a) Comparison of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC in dark adsorption and visible-light (λ > 420 nm)-induced photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI); (b) The pseudo-ﬁrst order kinetic plots for obtaining the photocatalytic reaction rate constants (k).
Mott-Schottky plots of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC for determining their flat band potentials.

(a) Photocatalytic reusability testing of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC; (b) XRD spectra of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC before and after photocatalytic use; (c) FT-IR spectra of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC before and after photocatalytic use; (d) Raman spectra of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC before and after photocatalytic use.

head.ce:title: Design and preparation of SnO2/SnS2/conjugated polyvinyl chloride derivative ternary composite with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physical Science and Technology, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Engineering and Science Program, Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH, 45221-0012, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental Engineering and Science Program, Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH 45221-0012, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new high-efficiency ternary composite photocatalyst (SnO2/SnS2/conjugated polyvinyl chloride derivative (CPVC)) was designed based on the hypothesis that any two of its components can constitute type-II heterojunction, and its three components should have synergistic effect on the enhancement of photoinduced charge separation. SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite was prepared adopting an easy-to-implement four-step synthesis method. Photoluminescence and electrochemical impedance spectra showed that SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is more efficient than SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC and SnS2 in the separation and transfer of photoinduced charges. Results from their photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under visible-light irradiation indicated that SnO2/SnS2/CPVC has significantly higher visible-light photocatalytic activity than SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, SnS2, CPVC and SnO2. Moreover, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC also demonstrated good photocatalytic reusability and stability. This study not only has developed a new high-activity visible-light photocatalyst (SnO2/SnS2/CPVC), but is also useful for the design of other new ternary composite photocatalysts with synergistically enhanced visible-light photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Our previous studies demonstrated that the photocatalytic activity of SnS2 can be considerably improved by coupling with SnO2 [20] or CPVC (conjugated polyene derivative from thermal dehydrochlorination of polyvinyl chloride (PVC)) [21]. In the SnO2/SnS2 composite, the conduction band (CB) potential of SnS2 is more negative than that of SnO2, whereas the valence band (VB) potential of SnO2 is more positive than that of SnS2 [20]. On the other hand, in the SnS2/CPVC composite, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) potential of CPVC is more negative than the CB potential of SnS2, whereas the VB potential of SnS2 is more positive than the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) potential of CPVC [21]. Hence, SnO2 and SnS2, and SnS2 and CPVC can constitute type-II heterojunctions as shown in Scheme 1 (a) and (b) [20,21], respectively. The type-II heterojunctions of SnO2/SnS2 and SnS2/CPVC can promote the separation of e− and h+ , resulting in their improved photocatalytic activities. Moreover, the coupling of SnS2 with CPVC can also increase the absorption of visible-light [21]. Besides, it has also been reported that CPVC/SnO2 composite has noticeable visible-light photocatalytic activity, owing to the transfer of e− from CPVC to SnO2 via the type-II heterojunction formed between CPVC and SnO2 (Scheme 1(c)) [22]. So, if SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are properly combined into a ternary composite, type-II heterojunction can be formed between any two components. Under visible-light excitation, the e− would transfer from CPVC to SnS2 to SnO2 as well as from CPVC to SnO2, whereas the h+ would transfer from SnS2 to CPVC (Scheme 1(d)). Accordingly, the three components of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC should have synergetic effect in enhancing the photoinduced charge separation. In addition, in the SnO2/SnS2/CPVC composite, SnO2 and CPVC would serve as the reactive sites for photocatalytic reduction and oxidation, respectively, which can suppress the photocorrosion and increase the lifetime of SnS2. Hence, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite would be expected to have better photocatalytic performance, in comparison with SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, SnS2 and SnO2. In this work, we have attempted the preparation and photocatalytic evaluation of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite, aiming to verify our design idea and develop a new high-activity visible-light photocatalyst.

SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite was prepared via the following four steps:
(1)
SnO2 nanoparticles were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of 3 mmol SnCl4·5H2O in 40 mL deionized water at 200 °C for 10 h;
(2)
SnO2/SnS2 nanocomposite was prepared via hydrothermal treatment of 3 mmol SnO2 nanoparticles and 15 mmol thioacetamide in 40 mL deionized water at 160 °C for 1 h;
(3)
An amount of 10 mg PVC powder was dissolved in 20 mL tetrahydrofuran, followed by the addition of 1000 mg SnO2/SnS2 nanocomposite. The resulting mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 150 min and ultrasonicated for 30 min, then heated at 60 °C for 6 h for the evaporation of tetrahydrofuran. So, SnO2/SnS2/PVC composite was prepared; and
(4)
SnO2/SnS2/CPVC composite was prepared by heating SnO2/SnS2/PVC at 150 °C for 2 h in air atmosphere.

For comparison, SnS2 was prepared via hydrothermal treatment of 3 mmol SnO2 and 15 mmol thioacetamide in 40 mL deionized water at 160 °C for 10 h. CPVC was prepared by heating 500 mg PVC at 150 °C for 2 h in air atmosphere.

The composition and structure of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by use of Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi XPS system, FEI Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN field-emission transmission electron microscope, Thermo DXR GX-PT-2412 Raman spectroscopy (the adopted excitation wavelength is 532 nm laser), Bruker Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer, and Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE X-Ray Diffractometer. The electrochemical impedance spectra were measured in the aqueous solution of 0.1 mol/L KCl and 5 mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−, using a Bio-Logic VMP3 electrochemical workstation. The three electrodes used included the reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode), counter electrode (platinum rod), and working electrode (glassy carbon electrode coated with the as-prepared sample). Mott-Schottky plots were measured in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution, using a CHI600D electrochemical workstation. The three electrodes used included the reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode), counter electrode (platinum rod), and working electrode (ITO conductive glass coated with the as-prepared sample). Photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared samples (300 mg) were evaluated by the reduction of Cr(VI) in 300 mL 50 mg/L K2Cr2O7 aqueous solution under visible-light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation. The instrument and detailed procedures for the photocatalytic tests are provided in the Supplementary materials.

The XRD spectra of the as-prepared SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC and CPVC are displayed in Fig. 1 (a). All the diffraction peaks appearing in the XRD spectrum of the as-prepared SnO2 correspond to tetragonal phase SnO2, whereas those appearing in the XRD spectrum of the as-prepared SnS2 correspond to hexagonal phase SnS2 [20]. CPVC shows no obvious diffraction peak, suggesting it is almost amorphous. The as-prepared SnO2/SnS2 and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC demonstrate similar XRD patterns, which comprise the diffraction peaks of both SnO2 and SnS2. Because of its low content and amorphous nature, CPVC shows no diffraction peak in the XRD spectrum of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC. Considering that FT-IR, Raman, and XPS are more sensitive to detect carbonaceous substances, they were further used to verify the presence of CPVC in the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC material.

The FT-IR spectra of SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnO2/SnS2/CPVC and CPVC are presented in Fig. 1(b). SnO2 shows obvious FT-IR peaks at about 530, 665, 1635, and 3430 cm−1, which can be assigned to the symmetric stretching vibration of SnO, asymmetric stretching vibration of OSnO, bending vibration of OH of crystal or adsorbed water, and stretching vibration of OH of crystal or adsorbed water [23,24], respectively. SnS2 shows no salient FT-IR peaks. CPVC displays many FT-IR peaks of PVC, including CCl stretching vibrations (614 and 693 cm−1), CH2 in-plane deformation (962 cm−1), CC skeleton bending vibration (1099 cm−1), CHCl stretching vibrations (1254 and 1334 cm−1), CH2 CHCl symmetric stretching vibration (1428 cm−1), and CH2 stretching vibrations (2916 and 2973 cm−1) [22,25]. In addition, the weak peaks corresponding to conjugated polyene (such as CC stretching vibration at about 1605 cm−1, and CH stretching vibration at about 3037 cm−1) also appear in the FT-IR spectrum of CPVC [22,25]. In the FT-IR spectrum of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, the FT-IR peaks of CPVC and SnO2/SnS2 are clearly visible, suggesting CPVC and SnO2/SnS2 coexist in the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC material. Furthermore, as can be seen from the Raman spectra in Fig. 1(c), the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC shows not only the Raman peaks of SnS2 (311 cm−1 (A1g)) and SnO2 (571 cm−1 (B1u) and 630 cm−1 (A1g)), but also the Raman peaks of CPVC at about 1519 cm−1 (conjugated polyene vibration) and 1128 cm−1 (CC vibration) [21,24].

The XPS analysis results of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC are presented in Fig. 2 . From the survey XPS spectrum, it can be identified that the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is made up of Sn, O, S, C and Cl elements. The Sn 3d XPS spectrum shows the presence of two peaks at approximately 495.3 and 486.8 eV, which correspond to 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 of Sn4+ [26–28], respectively. The O 1s XPS spectrum has been resolved into two peaks at 532.8 and 531.3 eV, which correspond to hydroxyl O and lattice O of nanocrystalline SnO2 [26–28], respectively. The S 2p XPS spectrum can be fitted into two peaks at 162.9 and 161.7 eV, which correspond to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of S2– [26–28], respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum has been resolved into two peaks at 284.8 and 286.5 eV, which may be assigned to C=C/C–C/C–H and C–O/C–Cl [21], respectively. The Cl 2p XPS spectrum has been fitted into two peaks at 201.4 and 199.9 eV, which are in turn ascribed to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of the residual Cl in CPVC [21]. Thus, the above XRD, FT-IR, Raman and XPS analyses confirmed the preparation of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite.

The microstructures of the as-prepared SnO2, SnS2 and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC were observed with the aid of TEM and HRTEM. The TEM images in Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show that SnO2 consists of nanoparticles with sizes of about 7–15 nm, whereas SnS2 comprises nanosheets with lateral sizes of up to 625 nm. From the TEM image in Fig. 3(c), it can be observed that the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite comprised nanosheets decorated with smaller nanoparticles. In the HRTEM image in Fig. 3(d), both the (100) lattice fringes (d =0.316 nm) of hexagonal phase SnS2 and the (110) lattice fringes (d =0.335 nm) of tetragonal phase SnO2 can be clearly seen. The heterojunction structure was formed between SnO2 and SnS2, which would facilitate the interfacial charge transfer. Nevertheless, because CPVC is almost amorphous and its content is rather low (less than 1%), it is difficult to identify precisely the locations of CPVC in Fig. 3(d). Probably, the hazy amorphous substance on the surfaces and edges of SnS2 nanosheets and SnO2 nanocrystallites is CPVC.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of SnO2, SnS2, CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC were determined and transformed into the absorbance spectra in Fig. 4 (a) by use of the Kubelka-Munk formula (Eqs. (1) and (2)) [29]:
(1) F(R∞) = (1 – R∞)2/2R∞ = α/S = AC/S
(2) R∞ = R(Sample)/R(BaSO4)
where F(R∞), R, α, S, A and C symbolize the Kubelka-Munk function, reflectance, absorption coefficient, scattering coefficient, absorbance and concentration of the absorbing species, respectively. As seen from Fig. 4(a), SnO2 demonstrates no absorption of visible-light, whereas SnS2, CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC all show obvious absorption of visible-light. According to the Tauc plots as shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), the Eg values of SnO2, SnS2, CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC were determined to be 3.49, 1.98, 1.87 and 2.17 eV, respectively.

Fig. 5 (a) demonstrates the photoluminescence spectra of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC under the excitation of 532 nm laser at room temperature. Apparently, all the four samples show a photoluminescence peak at about 541 nm, which may originate from the radiative excitonic recombination of SnS2. Nevertheless, the photoluminescence peak intensity of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is the lowest, suggesting co-modification with SnO2 and CPVC plays synergistic roles in reducing the recombination of e– and h + of SnS2. Besides, as seen from the electrochemical impedance spectra of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC in Fig. 5(b), SnO2/SnS2/CPVC shows the smallest Nyquist arc radius, suggesting its three components have synergistic effect in lowering the interfacial charge transfer resistance and accelerating the interfacial charge transfer. The enhanced separation and transfer of photoinduced charges of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC can contribute to its improved photocatalytic activity [30–33].

Fig. 6 (a) demonstrates the performance of CPVC, SnO2, SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC in dark adsorption and visible-light (λ > 420 nm)-induced photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI). As can be seen from Fig. 6(a), under the dark condition, the adsorption of Cr(VI) by different samples can reach equilibrium after 80 min, because the Cr(VI) concentration remained unchanged when the adsorption time was extended from 80 to 100 min. Once the visible-light irradiation source was on, the Cr(VI) concentration continually decreased with the prolonging of irradiation time in the presence of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, suggesting all these photocatalytic materials had visible-light photocatalytic activity for Cr(VI) reduction. However, SnO2 and CPVC showed no photocatalytic activity for Cr(VI) reduction under visible-light irradiation. SnO2 has no visible-light photocatalytic activity because it cannot absorb visible-light due to its large bandgap (Fig. 4). On the other hand, CPVC has no visible-light photocatalytic activity because its photoinduced electron-hole pairs undergo complete recombination [21]. SnO2/SnS2/CPVC showed the best performance in photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). In the presence of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, Cr(VI) concentration was reduced to nearly zero after 120 min visible-light irradiation. For quantitative comparison of the photocatalytic activities of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, the reaction rate constants (k) for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) over these catalysts were obtained by use of the pseudo-first order kinetic equation (Eq. (3)) [34–36]:
(3) ln(Ci0/Cit) = kti
where Ci0 and Cit refer to Cr(VI) concentration at the irradiation time of 0 and t min, respectively, and ti is the irradiation time. According to the pseudo-ﬁrst order kinetic plots as shown in Fig. 6(b), the obtained k values in the presence of SnS2, SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC, SnO2/CPVC, and SnO2/SnS2/CPVC are 0.003, 0.011, 0.006, 0.001, and 0.029 min–1, respectively. Apparently, the photocatalytic activity of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC far surpasses those of SnS2 and binary composites (SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/CPVC).

To support the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite proposed in Scheme 1(d), the band structures of its three components were also determined. The measured Mott-Schottky plots of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are given in Fig. 7 , from which the flat band potentials of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC were determined to be –0.126, –0.376 and –0.483 V vs. SCE (that is, +0.115, –0.135 and –0.242 V vs. NHE), respectively. Furthermore, from the positive slopes of the straight-line portions of their Mott-Schottky plots, it can be inferred that SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC belong to n-type semiconductors [37–39]. It is assumed that the conduction band (CB) potentials of n-type semiconductors are more negative than their flat band potentials by 0.1 V [37–39]. Therefore, the CB potentials of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are +0.015, –0.235 and –0.342 V vs. NHE, respectively. The valence band (VB) potentials of these materials can be calculated according to Eq. (4):
(4) EVB = ECB + Eg

The VB potentials of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are +3.505, +1.745 and +1.528 V vs. NHE, given that the Eg values of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are 3.49, 1.98 and 1.87 eV (Fig. 4), respectively. The determined band structures of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are shown in Scheme 2 . Obviously, the CB potentials as well as the VB potentials of SnO2, SnS2 and CPVC are in descending order (it is noted that for CPVC, the CB and VB represent the LUMO and HOMO, respectively). This is consistent with Scheme 1(d). Therefore, SnO2 and SnS2, SnS2 and CPVC, and SnO2 and SnS2 all can form type-II heterojunctions due to their matched band structures. When SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is irradiated by visible-light (λ > 420 nm), only visible-light-absorbing SnS2 and CPVC can be excited, producing e– and h +. Driven by the electric potential difference, the e− would transfer from CPVC to SnS2 to SnO2 as well as from CPVC to SnO2, whereas the h+ would transfer from SnS2 to CPVC (Scheme 1(d)). Such photoinduced charge transfer has synergistic effect in enhancing the separation of e– and h + (Fig. 5), resulting in the exceptionally improved photocatalytic activity of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC.

For a new photocatalyst, its reusability and stability are of primary concerns. Therefore, the reusability and stability of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC photocatalyst were also investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 8 (a) that the photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC shows only slight decline as the number of usage cycle increases. At the end of the third cycle, above 90% of Cr(VI) can still be reduced. The slight performance decline is likely caused by the deposition of Cr(OH)3 on the surface active sites of the photocatalyst in the previous photocatalytic cycles [40–42]. Besides, as demonstrated by Fig. 8(b), (c) and (d), the XRD, FT-IR and Raman spectra of the sample recovered after photocatalytic use are almost the same as those of the freshly prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC, indicating SnO2/SnS2/CPVC is fairly stable for photocatalytic applications. The far higher photocatalytic activity and good stability of the as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC indicate that our design idea and preparation method of this new ternary composite photocatalyst are effective and practical. Moreover, the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism proposed in Scheme 1(d) may also be applicable to develop other new ternary composite photocatalysts with synergistically enhanced visible-light photocatalysis.

Based on the hypothesis that any two of its components can constitute type-II heterojunction, and its three components should have synergistic effect in enhancing the photoinduced charge separation, a new ternary composite photocatalyst (SnO2/SnS2/CPVC) was designed. An easy-to-implement four-step method was adopted to prepare SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite from low cost and easily accessible SnCl4·5H2O, thioacetamide and PVC. The formation of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC ternary composite was confirmed by XRD, FT-IR, Raman, XPS and HRTEM analyses. The as-prepared SnO2/SnS2/CPVC showed not only much higher visible-light photocatalytic activity than those of SnS2 and binary composites (SnO2/SnS2, SnS2/CPVC and SnO2/CPVC), but also good photocatalytic reusability and stability. Hence, our design idea and preparation method of SnO2/SnS2/CPVC photocatalyst are effective and practical, and may also be useful for developing other new ternary composite photocatalysts with synergistically enhanced visible-light photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171282), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2019.110524.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of visible-light-responsive morphology-controlled brookite TiO2 nanorods by site-selective loading of AuAg bimetallic nanoparticles
Appl. Catal. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ma, K. Kobayashi, Y. Cao, T. Ohno, Development of visible-light-responsive morphology-controlled brookite TiO2 nanorods by site-selective loading of AuAg bimetallic nanoparticles, Appl. Catal. B 245 (2019) 681-690.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) on hematite nanoparticles in the presence of oxalate and citrate
Appl. Catal. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Kretschmer, A.M. Senn, J.M. Meichtry, G. Custo, M.I. Litter, Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) on hematite nanoparticles in the presence of oxalate and citrate, Appl. Catal. B 242 (2019) 218-226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and characterization of black TiO2 with different Ti3+ concentrations under atmospheric conditions
J. Catal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, S. Yuan, Q. Zhang, B. Xu, T. Ohno, Fabrication and characterization of black TiO2 with different Ti3+ concentrations under atmospheric conditions, J. Catal. 366 (2018) 282-288.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coral-inspired nanoscale design of porous SnS2 for photocatalytic reduction and removal of aqueous Cr (VI)
Appl. Catal. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Qu, D. Chen, N. Li, Q. Xu, J. Lu, Coral-inspired nanoscale design of porous SnS2 for photocatalytic reduction and removal of aqueous Cr (VI), Appl. Catal. B 207 (2017) 404-411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size-tunable hydrothermal synthesis of SnS2 nanocrystals with high performance in visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI)
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.C. Zhang, J. Li, M. Zhang, D.D. Dionysiou, Size-tunable hydrothermal synthesis of SnS2 nanocrystals with high performance in visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI), Environ. Sci. Technol. 45 (2011) 9324-9331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A direct Z-scheme g-C3N4/SnS2 photocatalyst with superior visible-light CO2 reduction performance
J. Catal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Di, B. Zhu, B. Cheng, J. Yu, Jingsan Xu, A direct Z-scheme g-C3N4/SnS2 photocatalyst with superior visible-light CO2 reduction performance, J. Catal. 352 (2017) 532-541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide over SnS2
Mater. Lett.
